python.explanation:
The algorithm follows classic minimax recursion with three distinct cases. First, if the current node is terminal, return its known exact value immediately — this takes priority over depth limits because we have ground truth. Second, if depth is zero or the node has no children, fall back to the learned linear value function V(s) = φ(s)ᵀw via a dot product between the node's feature vector and the weight vector. Third, for interior nodes within budget, recurse into all children, alternating between max and min players.

The alternating player logic mirrors zero-sum game theory: the maximizer picks the child with the highest value (best outcome for them), while the minimizer picks the child with the lowest value (worst outcome for the maximizer, best for them). This back-propagation of values up the tree produces the minimax optimal strategy.

The learned value function acts as a heuristic that approximates the true game-theoretic value of states that are too deep to fully expand. This is the core idea behind systems like AlphaGo/AlphaZero, where a neural network replaces hand-crafted evaluation functions. The linear model here is the simplest form: a dot product of engineered features with learned weights.

Time complexity is O(b^d) where b is branching factor and d is depth, matching standard minimax. Space complexity is O(d) for the recursion stack. In practice, alpha-beta pruning reduces the effective branching factor, but that optimization is not required here.
key_learnings: Terminal node check must come before depth-limit check — exact values always override approximations, regardless of remaining depth budget.
The learned value function V(s) = φ(s)ᵀw is evaluated via np.dot(features[node], weights), bridging classical search with learned representations.
The 'no children' case (non-terminal leaf) is a subtle edge case: even with depth remaining, if there are no moves we must evaluate with the value function rather than return a sentinel.
Minimax alternates maximizing=True/False on recursive calls — passing 'not maximizing' implicitly handles two-player zero-sum without needing separate logic branches.
This pattern — depth-limited search + learned evaluator — is the foundation of AlphaGo, chess engines (Stockfish NNUE), and modern game AI; the linear model here generalizes to deep neural networks.

numpy.explanation:
The NumPy solution precomputes all leaf values with a single vectorized matrix-vector multiplication: `features @ weights` produces an (N,) array where entry i is V(node_i). This amortizes the dot product cost across all nodes in one BLAS call instead of computing it on-demand per leaf visit — beneficial when many nodes share the same features array and the tree is wide.

The inner `_minimax` closure captures the precomputed `all_values` array and uses standard index lookup O(1) at leaves instead of recomputing the dot product each time. At interior nodes, child values are collected into a NumPy array so `np.max`/`np.min` can operate on them vectorially — though with small branching factors the benefit is marginal, it scales well for high-branching games.

This pattern of 'precompute once, index many times' is a general NumPy optimization principle. When the same computation would be repeated across many recursive calls with different indices, batching upfront with matrix operations avoids Python-level loop overhead per call.

The trade-off is memory: storing all_values requires O(N) space even if only a small fraction of nodes are visited. For very large game graphs this may not be worthwhile, but for moderately sized trees (N up to millions) it's a clean win.
key_learnings: Precomputing `features @ weights` once via BLAS matrix-vector multiply is faster than repeated per-node dot products when many leaves are evaluated.
Closures in Python capture outer variables by reference — `all_values` is shared efficiently across all recursive calls without copying.
np.max/np.min over a small array of child values is marginally faster than Python's built-in max/min due to reduced Python object overhead.
The precompute-then-index pattern is fundamental NumPy style: transform repeated computation into a single batch operation followed by O(1) lookups.
For large-scale MCTS or minimax, batching leaf evaluations with a neural network (forward pass over many nodes at once) follows this same principle and is how AlphaZero achieves GPU efficiency.
